coredata.dc:title: Cancer cell identification by facile imaging of intracellular reductive substances with fluorescent nanosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunrong
Xu, Yuzhi
Liu, Si-Yang
Zhang, Yanfei
Yin, Wen
Dai, Zong
Zou, Xiaoyong
coredata.dc:description: Ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH), the most abundant intracellular reductive substances, have been widely used as biomarkers for cancer cells identification. The current methods relying on imaging of AA or GSH alone to identify cancer cells may cause systematic errors, since a mutual conversion relationship exists between AA and GSH. In this work, we propose a fluorescent nanosensor for the simultaneous imaging of intracellular reductive substances including AA and GSH. Biocompatible fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) with rich surface amine and carboxyl groups were synthesized. The fluorescence of the SiNP was initially quenched by chelation of Fe3+ ions, forming SiNP/Fe3+ complex as the fluorescent nanosensor. Upon the redox reaction with reductive substances, the nanosensor showed sensitively fluorescent recovery. Moreover, benefited from the efficient cellular uptake of the SiNP/Fe3+ and the overexpressed intracellular reductive substances in cancer cells, the fluorescent nanosensor was used to accurately identify the human breast carcinoma (MCF-7) cells from normal mammary epithelial (MCF-10A) cells by imaging of intracellular AA and GSH simultaneously. This strategy would be promising in imaging-guided precision cancer diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Fluorescent sensor
Cell identification
Reductive substance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AA and GSH detection by SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor
Principle of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor for cancer cells identification
In vitro AA and GSH assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Technology Project of Shenzhen City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21775169 , 21801259 , 21974153 , 21675180 ), the Scientific Technology Project of Shenzhen City ( JCYJ20200109142410170 ), the Scientific Technology Project of Guangzhou City ( 202103000003 ), the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation ( 2018A030313290 , 2019A1515010587 ), and the Science and Technology Foundation of Guizhou Province ( 2019–1301 , 20201Y051 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of SiNPs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SiNPs, and fluorescence of SiNPs (λex = 360 nm, λem = 430 nm). (c) FT-IR spectrum of SiNPs. (d) XPS full scan of SiNPs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of 50 μg mL−1 SiNPs under a series of concentrations of Fe3+ (0–1000 μM). (b) Quenching efficiency of SiNPs in the presence of Fe3+. (c) Dynamic fluorescence quenching of 50 μg mL−1 SiNPs in the presence of 500 μM Fe3+. (d) Specificity of the SiNPs toward Fe3+ against other ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SiNP/Fe3+ upon addition of AA at different concentrations. (b) Recovery efficiency of SiNP/Fe3+ in the presence of various concentrations of AA and the linear relationship between the recovery efficiency and the concentrations of AA (inset). (c) Fluorescence spectra of SiNP/Fe3+ upon addition of GSH at different concentrations. (d) Recovery efficiency of SiNP/Fe3+ in the presence of various concentrations of GSH and the linear relationship between the recovery efficiency and the concentrations of GSH (inset).
(a) Recovery efficiency of SiNP/Fe3+ upon the different biomolecules (500 μM). (b) Recovery efficiency of SiNP/Fe3+ upon different concentrations of ions (500 μM).

(a) Selectivity of the SiNP/Fe3+ probe (SiNPs 50 μg mL−1, Fe3+ 500 μM) for AA/GSH over other reductive substances and the common anions (a: AA, b: GSH, c: Catalase 200 u mL−1, d: Vitamin E, e: Bilirubin, f: Glucose, g: Uric acid, h: L-glutamic, i: Glycine (500 μM), j: Hydrogen peroxide, k: ClO−, l: NO3 −, m: HCO3 −, n: HPO4 2− (500 μM)). (b) The optical stability of SiNPs in different time.
Fluorescence images of SiNP/Fe3+ in (a) MCF-7 cancer cells and (b) MCF-10A normal cells. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells and MCF-10A cells with different treatments and then incubated with SiNP/Fe3+. (a) MCF-7 cells treated with NEM. (b) MCF-10A cells treated with LPA. (c) MCF-7 cancer cells and (d) MCF-10A normal cells treated with AA. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Fluorescence images of SiNP/Fe3+ in MCF-7 cancer cells (pretreated with Calcein-AM) and MCF-10A normal cells co-culture. The white circle and blue irregular shape are used to mark the position of normal cells and cancer cells, respectively. Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence quenching and recovery progresses of SiNPs under Fe3+ and reductive substance, respectively. (b) Fluorescence imaging of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor in normal and cancer cells. (c) Identification of normal/cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Cancer cell identification by facile imaging of intracellular reductive substances with fluorescent nanosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, 510275, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, 510275, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qiannan Medical College for Nationalities, Duyun, 558000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qiannan Medical College for Nationalities, Duyun, 558000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH), the most abundant intracellular reductive substances, have been widely used as biomarkers for cancer cells identification. The current methods relying on imaging of AA or GSH alone to identify cancer cells may cause systematic errors, since a mutual conversion relationship exists between AA and GSH. In this work, we propose a fluorescent nanosensor for the simultaneous imaging of intracellular reductive substances including AA and GSH. Biocompatible fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) with rich surface amine and carboxyl groups were synthesized. The fluorescence of the SiNP was initially quenched by chelation of Fe3+ ions, forming SiNP/Fe3+ complex as the fluorescent nanosensor. Upon the redox reaction with reductive substances, the nanosensor showed sensitively fluorescent recovery. Moreover, benefited from the efficient cellular uptake of the SiNP/Fe3+ and the overexpressed intracellular reductive substances in cancer cells, the fluorescent nanosensor was used to accurately identify the human breast carcinoma (MCF-7) cells from normal mammary epithelial (MCF-10A) cells by imaging of intracellular AA and GSH simultaneously. This strategy would be promising in imaging-guided precision cancer diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensitive and accurate identification of cancer cells from normal ones is of great significance in tumor diagnosis and therapeutics [1–3]. Normally, the surface specific proteins, intracellular nucleic acids, enzymes, and hormone are adopted as cancer biomarkers for imaging [4–7]. It has been reported that the intracellular redox status plays vital roles in many cellular processes, such as protein folding, transcriptional activation, DNA synthesis, and enzyme kinetics [8]. Cancer cells contain more radicals and reactive oxygen species comparing to normal cells. Thus, large amounts of reducing substances, including catalase, vitamin E, ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), etc., will be produced to neutralize these oxidative substances [9,10], resulting in a much higher concentration of reducing substances in cancer cells [11–13]. Therefore, the intracellular reducing substance (IRS) has been used as a new indicator for cancer/normal cells identification [14].

Among IRSs, GSH and AA are most abundant, while the other reducing substances, such as radical quenching or scavenging proteins, vitamin E, bilirubin, etc., are with either relatively low reducing capacity or low concentration [15–17]. In human body, the concentration of intracellular GSH is in the millimolar range. The concentration of GSH in cancer cell is about 4-fold higher as compared to the corresponding normal cell [18–20]. In addition, AA is also with relatively high intracellular concentration in the micromolar range (1.51–11.2 μM) [21–23]. To date, most of the studies reported the identification of cancer cell by imaging of AA or GSH alone, which was achieved by the specific oxidization of indicators by AA or GSH to produce fluorescent substances [24]. However, when AA is oxidized, GSH rapidly reduces the intracellular dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) to AA [25], resulting in a mutual conversion relationship between AA and GSH. Therefore, identification of cancer cells by imaging of AA or GSH alone will cause systematic errors and provide false-positive results. Detecting the total amount of IRSs, particularly the simultaneous detection of AA and GSH, is expected to be greatly significant for the accurate identification of cancer cells. However, there have not been any related methods reported till now.

Herein, we propose a facile fluorescent nanosensor for the sensitively and specifically simultaneous imaging of intracellular GSH and AA. It is known that silicon nanoparticle (SiNP) has been widely used as biosensor for cell imaging due to its unique optical stability and low cytotoxicity [26]. We synthesized the fluorescent SiNPs with abundant surface amino and carboxyl groups. Then, the Fe3+ ion, a strong oxidizing ion that can quench the fluorescence of SiNP and also can react with reductive substances, was chelated on the SiNP to construct the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor. In the presence of AA and GSH, the fluorescence of the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor can be sensitively recovered due to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. The feasibility of the nanosensor for cancer cell identification was demonstrated by using MCF-7 cancer cells and MCF-10A normal cells as models.

N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), α-lipoic alcohol (LPA), Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), and 3-(trimethoxysilyl)-1-propanamine (APTMS), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). FeCl3·6H2O, AA, GSH, histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), phenylalanie (Phen), dopamine (DA), lysine (Lys), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), vitamin E, bilirubin, catalase, glucose, uric acid, L-glutamic, and glycine were obtained from Aladdin (China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) were obtained from Gibco (USA). AA assay kit and GSH assay kit were purchased from Solarbio (China). MTT was obtained from Beyotime (China). Ultrapure water was used in all the experiments (18.2 MΩ cm−1, Millipore Co., USA).

The analysis of MTT assay was carried out on a Max M2-R microplate reader (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence was measured on a F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-2750 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime spectra was collected on a FLS980 series of spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., England). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on an Nexus 670 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired on a Nexsa X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was acquired on a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands).

EDTA (0.2 g) and APTMS (1.0 mL) were mixed and then sonicated for 30 min to obtain a clear solution. Then the obtained solution was heated for 5 min in a microwave oven (500 W) with a frequency of 2450 MHz, and yielding a white solid. The white solid was cooled and diluted with 4 mL deionized water, and then centrifuged at 8000 r min−1 for 20 min to remove the non-fluorescent materials. The obtained supernatant was dialyzed using dialysis membrane (cut off: 1 kDa) for 24 h. Then the SiNPs solution was rotary evaporated and dried at 55 °C for 24 h to obtain SiNPs.

The SiNPs were dissolved in 1.0 mL of deoxidized deionized water with a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1. Metal ion stock aqueous solutions containing Fe3+ (adding 1–2 drops of HCl solution (1 M), pH 1.3), Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, Cr3+ and Mn2+ were prepared. By diluting the stock solution with deoxidized deionized water, FeCl3 solutions with different concentrations (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 50, 100, 200, 500, 800, and 1000 μM) were obtained. The SiNPs solution was mixed with the FeCl3 solution, and the SiNPs with a final concentration of 50 μg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AA and GSH detection by SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Five-μL of 1.0 mg mL–1 SiNPs solution was mixed with 5 μL of 10 mM Fe3+ solution in the 100 μL centrifuge tubes for incubation. AA and GSH solutions were added into the 100 μL tubes, respectively. The mixture was diluted with deoxidized deionized [1–43] water to 100 μL and equilibrated for 30 min. Their fluorescence emission spectra were measured with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were obtained from Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences of Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS, 100 U mL–1 penicillin, and 100 μg mL–1 streptomycin. All cells were cultured at 37 °C in 5% CO2 and 95% air.

MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were seeded and cultured in a 96-well plate with a cell density of 1 × 104 cells per well in 100 μL of DMEM for 24 h. Then 100 μL of fresh cell medium that containing different concentrations of SiNP/Fe3+ solution was added into. After incubation for 24 h, 10 μL MTT solution was added into per well and cultured for 4 h. Then DMEM medium was discharged and replaced with 200 μL of DMSO. After incubation for 4 h, the absorbance at 570 nm was measured by a microplate reader.

MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were dispersed in chamber slides separately, and cultured at 37 °C in 5% CO2 and 95% air overnight. After that, the medium was discharged and replaced with SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor in culture medium for 3 h incubation. After being washed 3 times with cold PBS, the cells imaging was performed with a LSM 710 confocal microscopy (Carl Zeiss, Germany).

The MCF-7 cells were pretreated with 10 μM NEM for 20 min, followed by incubation with SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor for 3 h. The MCF-10A cells were pretreated with 400 μM LPA for overnight. For co-culture assay, the MCF-7 cells were stained with 1.0 μM Calcein-AM (green) for 20 min and washed 3 times with PBS, then the MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were co-cultured for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor for cancer cells identification
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the coordination with the amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of SiNPs, Fe3+ can highly efficiently and selectively chelate with SiNPs. Ascribing to the charge properties of Fe3+, the fluorescence of SiNPs is quenched by the Fe3+, forming the SiNP/Fe3+ complex as the fluorescent nanosensor. When SiNP/Fe3+ reacts with reductive substances, such as AA and GSH, the Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ that possesses much lower affinity and quenching ability towards SiNPs, so the fluorescence of SiNPs can be recovered [27,28]. As shown in Scheme 1 . APTMS was chosen as silicon precursor because it has amino group, which has a vital role to the synthesis of water-soluble SiNPs. EDTA was used as cross-linker because it has abundant carboxyl group. Due to the high affinity and fast chelating process between the amino and carboxyl groups and Fe3+, as such, the fluorescence quenching is caused by the electron transfer in the formed complexes between SiNPs and Fe3+ [29]. In addition, Fe3+ has a high charge/radius ratio, thus, the amino and carboxyl groups on SiNPs can coordinate Fe3+ efficiently, resulting in the much higher fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ than other ions [30]. Among the metal ion quenchers used in fluorescent biosensors, Fe3+ is preferred due to its nontoxicity and strong oxidizing compared to the heavy metal ions involved fluorescent biosensors. The SiNP/Fe3+ complex can be decomposed with reductive substances, such as AA and GSH. During this recovery process, the Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by reductive substances. Fe2+ possesses much lower affinity and quenching ability towards SiNPs, so the fluorescence of SiNPs can be recovered [31]. As the SiNPs with small size can be easily uptaken by cells without any transfer reagents, the SiNP/Fe3+nanosensor can efficiently identify the cancer cells from normal cells at single-cell level, because cancer and normal cells show different intracellular redox status [32]. Furthermore, the SiNPs possess low cytotoxicity and great bio-degradability [33,34]. These properties make the SiNP/Fe3+as a promising label-free fluorescence nanosensor for biological applications.

The SiNPs were synthesized with EDTA and APTMS by microwave pyrolysis method, and their morphology and structure were characterized [35]. From TEM images, the SiNPs with diameters of ±3.8 nm dispersed uniformly without apparent aggregation (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S1) [36,37]. The absorption peak of SiNPs aqueous solution focuses at 360 nm, which also indicates that the SiNPs are well dispersed with uniform size [38]. Besides, the SiNPs showed strong fluorescence emission with a maximum emission wavelength of 430 nm upon the excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 1b). From the inset of Fig. 1b, under irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp, the SiNPs exhibited strong blue fluorescence, demonstrating that the SiNPs showed excellent dispersion in aqueous solution and luminescence performances.

The presence of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of SiNPs were then verified. As presented in Fig. 1c, the FT-IR spectrum of SiNPs shows clear peaks of the N–H bending vibrations at 1595 cm −1, the peak at 1650 cm −1 shows the CO vibrational absorption, and the stretching vibrations of-NH2 and –OH groups at 3100–3500 cm −1. Moreover, XPS was performed for the chemical composition and valence state analysis of SiNPs. As shown in Fig. 1d and Fig. S2, the XPS full scan spectrum showed that SiNPs contains four elements, C (37.4%), O (21.8%), N (14.9%), and Si (25.6%), and presents four predominant peaks at 102.31, 284.79, 398.99, and 531.98, eV, corresponding to the binding energies of Si 2p, C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively.

The high-resolution of Si 2p, C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra further confirm that the Si–O, Si–O–C, C–C, C–N/C–O, C–O, C–N–C, and C–NH2 bonds are exhibit in the SiNPs. In general, the FT-IR and XPS analysis demonstrate that the synthesized SiNPs possess rich -NH2 and –COOH groups which are advantageous for the chelation with Fe3+ ions and dispersion of SiNPs in aqueous solution.

To develop a fluorescent nanosensor that can response to reductive substances based on SiNPs, 5 μL of SiNPs solutions (1.0 mg mL–1) were separately added with various concentrations of Fe3+ and incubated for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 2 a, when a series of Fe3+ solutions (0–1000 μM) were added into the SiNPs solutions, the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs gradually decreased, and finally reached a steady state, forming the SiNP/Fe3+ complex. With the addition of Fe3+ (500 μM), the quenching efficiency ((F 0–F)/F 0, where F and F 0 are fluorescence intensities of the SiNPs at 430 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively) reached a maximum at ~70% (Fig. 2b). Therefore, 500 μM of Fe3+ was employed in the subsequent experiments to reduce the background interference. In addition, the quenching kinetics of the fluorescent nanosensor were inspected (Fig. 2c). With the addition of Fe3+, about 57.4% quenching efficiency was obtained at initial reaction of SiNP/Fe3+. The interaction of the Fe3+ with SiNPs was a quite fast process that reached to equilibrium in about 1000 s, and the quenching efficiency was approximately 72.5%. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2d, comparing to Fe3+, other ions (including Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, Cr3+ and Mn2+) barely quenched the fluorescence of SiNPs. In addition, with the addition of other ions with Fe3+, the quenching efficiency was not changed. In general, the fluorescence of SiNPs can be selectively and efficiently quenched by Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S3a, the absorption peak was slightly blue-shifted from 250 to 245 nm and the absorbance was also reduced with the addition of Fe3+, indicating that Fe3+ can interact with SiNPs and affect the charge transfer process on SiNPs [39]. Besides, after the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescent lifetime of the SiNP/Fe3+ was 0.39 ns, which is almost the same as that of SiNPs (0.42 ns) (Fig. S3b), excluding the existence of dynamic quenching. And the fluorescence quenching of the SiNPs by Fe3+ followed a typical Stern-Volmer equation, with a quenching constant of 7.6 × 104 M −1 (Fig. S4) [40]. The unchanged fluorescence lifetime and large quenching constant indicate that the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ to SiNPs should be static quenching [41,42].

The pH can commonly influence the fluorescence of nanosensor, and it is varied in different physiological environments. Therefore, the stability of the SiNPs and the sensing performance of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor in different pH were investigated. As shown in Figs. S5a and b, the fluorescence of SiNPs and SiNP/Fe3+ kept stable in a wide pH range of 1–12, and fluorescence intensity of SiNPs was reduced in strength basic pH 13–14 because of the deprotonation of amino and dissociation of carboxyl groups. The recovered fluorescence of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor after adding AA and GSH (500 μM) was also stable in the pH range of 3–12 (Fig. S5c). The results indicate that SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor is stable to pH within physiological environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro AA and GSH assay
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely known that Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by reductive substances. Thus, the quenched fluorescence of SiNPs by Fe3+ might be recovered upon the addition of reductive substances. AA and GSH, the most abundant and reductive IRSs, were adopted for the proof-of-concept application. As shown in Fig. 3 a, with increasing the concentration of AA, the fluorescence intensity of the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor gradually increased. The recovery efficiency ((F–F 0)/F, F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensities of the SiNP/Fe3+ in the presence and absence of AA, respectively) was up to 73% in the presence of 5 mM AA (Fig. 3b). The recovery efficiency showed good linearity with the concentration of AA in the range of 0.1–5 μM, with linear regression equation of y = 14.36 + 8.72 x (R 2 = 0.95) (Fig. 3b, inset). The LOD was evaluated to be 21 nM (S/N = 3). The fluorescence of the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor gradually recovered with increasing the concentration of GSH (Fig. 3c), resulting in a recovery efficiency up to 68.6% at 10 mM GSH (Fig. 3d). Similarly, the recovery efficiency also showed good linearity with the concentration of GSH in the ranges of 1–20 μM and 50–500 μM, with linear regression equations of y = 9.37 + 1.31x (R 2 = 0.92) and y = 37.98 + 0.045 x (R 2 = 0.93), respectively (Fig. 3d, inset). The LOD was evaluated to be 352 nM and 16.7 μM. Compared to other methods of AA and GSH detection, our SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor exhibited relatively low LODs and wide linear ranges for AA and GSH detection. Besides, AA and GSH can be simultaneously detected by our method (Table S1). In addition, the concentration of AA and GSH in MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells lysis were determined to be 300–370 nM and 51–68 μM, respectively (Figs. S6a and b). According to the linear regression equations and the detection ranges of AA and GSH, the AA and GSH concentrations of cell lysis are within the linear range of this SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor. These results indicate that the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor can be used for the detection of AA and GSH in cells.

After confirmed the sensitivity, the selectivity of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor for the detection of reductive substance was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, compared with AA and GSH, the common interferential biomolecules, such as Cys, DA, Phe, His, Lys, Trp, and Tyr (500 μM), only induced a slight recovery of the fluorescence intensity of the SiNP/Fe3+ (2%–4%). Furthermore, the common ions, such as Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, Cr3+ and Mn2+ (500 μM), were added into SiNP/Fe3+ solutions, and the fluorescence intensity of SiNP/Fe3+ was barely changed (fluorescence response < 3%) (Fig. 4b). And then, the reductive substances were added into the above solutions, and these ions have negligible effect on the recovery of fluorescence of SiNP/Fe3+ by reductive substances. Furthermore, the selectivity for other reductive substances, such as catalase (200 u mL−1), vitamin E, bilirubin, glucose, uric acid, L-glutamic, glycine (500 μM), hydrogen peroxide, and the common anions, such as ClO−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, HPO4 2− (500 μM) were investigated. The recovery efficiencies are very low (only 0.7–4.2%) comparing to those of AA and GSH (Fig. 5 a). These results indicate that the fluorescence response of SiNP/Fe3+ to AA and GSH is highly selective. In summary, the developed SiNP/Fe3+ fluorescent nanosensor showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of AA and GSH.

Prior to the cell imaging, the stability of SiNPs in culture medium was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of SiNPs kept stable in 24 h, indicating that SiNPs are stable in culture medium. The low cytotoxicity and efficient cellular uptake are the key requirements for the intracellular imaging by nanosensor. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor was measured by the MTT assay. As presented in Figs. S7a and b, after 24 h incubation with SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor in the concentration range from 5 to 200 μg mL−1, the MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells viability maintained over 90%, demonstrating a satisfactory cytotoxicity of SiNP/Fe3+. Then, strong fluorescence signals of SiNPs were observed in MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells after the incubation with SiNPs, indicating an efficient cellular uptake of SiNPs (Fig. S8).

Next, the capability of the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor for the intracellular imaging of IRSs was verified. After incubating the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor (50 μg mL−1 SiNPs, 500 μM Fe3+) with cancer cells (MCF-7) for 3 h at 37 °C, strong fluorescence signal of SiNPs was observed in the cytoplasm, and the fluorescence intensity (FI) obtained from fluorescence channel of SiNPs was 0.82. On the contrary, after the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor was incubated with normal cells (MCF-10A) under the same conditions, only a weak fluorescence was observed and the FI was 0.23 (Fig. 6 a and b).

In order to explore the reasons for the difference between cancer cells and normal cells, the intracellular concentrations of IRSs, such as GSH and AA, were regulated. The MCF-7 cells were pretreated with 10 μM of NEM (a GSH scavenger to reduce the endogenous GSH) and the MCF-10A cells were pretreated with 400 μM of LPA (a GSH synthesis enhancer to up-regulate the endogenous GSH) [43]. Then, the treated and untreated MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were separately incubated with SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor for 3 h. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity of NEM-treated-MCF-7 cells was 22% of that untreated-MCF-7 cells. In contrast, a 2.8 times stronger fluorescence was observed in LPA-treated-MCF-10A cells comparing to untreated-MAF-10A cells (Fig. 7b). In addition, the intracellular concentration of AA was upregulated by the adding 40 μM of AA in cell culture medium. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, after AA-treatment, the fluorescence of SiNPs in MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells were enhanced by 1.3-fold and 3.4-fold compared the untreated ones, respectively. These results indicate that the difference of fluorescence intensity between cancer cells and normal cells should be ascribed to the difference in IRSs, and this SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor is highly sensitive to the change of IRSs.

Based on the excellent imaging performance for IRSs by SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor, the cancer cell identification by using SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor was further investigated. The MCF-7 cells were pretreated with Calcein-AM as green-fluorescent light (a fluorescent dye, was performed to stain living cells), and then co-cultured with MCF-10A cells. As presented in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence of SiNPs was only observed in MCF-7 cells, while barely observed in MCF-10A cells. These results confirm that SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor can be used to identify cancer cells (MCF-7) from the normal cells (MCF-10A) in a tumor environment-mimic situation, which makes SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor promising for the precision cancer diagnosis and therapy.

In summary, a fluorescent and biocompatible SiNP was synthesized and further integrated with Fe3+ to construct a fluorescent nanosensor, SiNP/Fe3+. The fluorescence of SiNP/Fe3+ was effectively and selectively recovered by reductive substances, because of the redox reaction between Fe3+ and reductive substance. Moreover, the SiNP/Fe3+ nanosensor possesses low cytotoxicity and efficient cellular uptake. Therefore, it can sensitively image the reductive substances in live cells for cancer cells identification. This facile and sensitive sensing platform based on SiNPs possesses great promise in cancer diagnosis and other related cell imaging.

Chunrong Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Original Draft. Si-yang Liu: Writing – Review& Editing. Yuzhi Xu: Writing – Review. Yanfei Zhang: Software, Formal analysis. Wen Yin: Writing – Review. Zong Dai: Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Resources. Xiaoyong Zou: Validation, Investigation, Data Curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21775169, 21801259, 21974153, 21675180), the Scientific Technology Project of Shenzhen City (JCYJ20200109142410170), the Scientific Technology Project of Guangzhou City (202103000003), the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2018A030313290, 2019A1515010587), and the Science and Technology Foundation of Guizhou Province (2019–1301, 20201Y051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122650.
